Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'm 28 and I've got a lot going on outside of school, so studying always felt like something I'd get to 'later.' I'd sometimes sit down with good intentions, maybe watch a video or two, but then days would pass before I'd look at course materials again. It was very scattered and reactive rather than planned.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then panic when I realized a deadline was coming and try to cram. Sometimes work stuff or personal issues would take over completely, and the course just fell off my radar. I never really established a consistent weekly rhythm.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd get an email reminder about something due, or I'd suddenly remember I hadn't checked in for a while and feel guilty. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I'd missed, scroll through feeling overwhelmed, and then log out without doing much. Fear of falling behind was the main motivator.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a week or two early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every few days. But it never stuck. Life would get in the way, and before I knew it, logging in felt more like a chore I was avoiding than something natural. The routine never formed.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. When I actually watched them, things clicked better than reading dense text. I appreciated being able to pause and rewind. The problem was I didn't watch them consistently enough to really benefit from them the way I should have.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums. They felt time-consuming and I was already behind, so adding more to my plate seemed impossible. The readings I mostly ignored too—I'd download them thinking I'd read later, but 'later' rarely came. Anything that required sustained effort got pushed aside.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could see an immediate payoff, like understanding a concept for an upcoming assignment. If it was going to directly help me not fail, I'd engage. But anything that felt abstract or 'nice to have' rather than essential? I just couldn't prioritize it with everything else going on.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—I was already struggling to complete the mandatory stuff. Required activities at least had deadlines that forced some action, even if I often missed them. Optional felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q9: Yes, I'd download PDFs and videos thinking I'd study them on my commute or at home without internet distractions. I had a folder full of course materials on my laptop. Sometimes I'd actually go through them, making notes on paper or just re-reading things. It wasn't always reflected in my LMS activity.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour or two with printed notes or downloaded slides, trying to understand concepts, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also think through problems in my head during downtime at work. A lot of my mental engagement with the material happened away from the computer entirely.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch sections offline when I had time. Or I'd access a reading, save it, and return to it days later without logging back in. The initial access was just me grabbing what I needed for later, not the actual studying.
Q12: Around midterm, everything got harder. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I was too far behind to catch up. Work got stressful, I was dealing with some personal stuff, and the course felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. The fear made me avoid it more, which made things worse. It was a bad cycle.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work, the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and face how behind I was. Confusion about what I'd missed made it harder to know where to start. I'd open the course, feel overwhelmed by the backlog, and close it again. Competing responsibilities always won.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video but not really absorb anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worried about other things, tired, distracted. I was going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. It was empty engagement.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material first before attempting them. That became an excuse to delay. The fear of doing poorly made me avoid starting. It's like I was scared to confront how much I didn't know, so I just... didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see something was due in two days and suddenly be in crisis mode. But by then it was often too late to do quality work. The deadlines created bursts of activity but not sustained engagement. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely.
Q17: When I actually submitted something and got feedback, it was helpful but also discouraging. Seeing what I'd done wrong made me feel more behind. After the CBT session I had, I tried to reframe feedback as useful rather than criticism, but honestly, I often avoided checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course had a lot of content spread across 18 modules, which felt overwhelming. I couldn't see a clear path through it all, especially once I fell behind. The structure assumed consistent engagement that I just wasn't providing. If it had been more modular or forgiving of irregular participation, maybe I'd have done better.
Q19: The sheer volume of materials made engagement harder. I'm visual, so I appreciated the videos, but there were so many that I couldn't keep up. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped. Also, more flexibility with deadlines might have let me catch up during better weeks.
Q20: I think that's fear and avoidance in action. You're logging in because you know you should, clicking around to feel productive, but you're too overwhelmed or scared to actually commit to the assignments. The activity is almost like procrastination—you're in the space but not doing the hard work. I recognize that pattern.
Q21: That doesn't really match my experience—I had low activity AND performed poorly. But I can imagine someone who's efficient, studies offline, and just logs in to submit. They might understand the material without needing constant LMS interaction. The system wouldn't capture their actual learning, just their minimal clicks.
Q22: Logs can show when you're present and what you click on, but they can't show what's going on in your head. They miss offline studying, they miss the emotional state you're in when engaging, and they definitely miss the fear and avoidance that keeps you from logging in at all. Presence isn't the same as learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—thinking about it, wrestling with it, wanting to understand. It's not just showing up or clicking through. For me, true engagement was rare this semester. I was more often just trying to survive than genuinely learning.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time. It wouldn't show the guilt I felt every time I avoided logging in, or the mental energy I spent worrying about the course even when I wasn't engaging with it. It wouldn't capture the offline attempts I made or how much life circumstances affected my ability to show up. The numbers just show absence, not the struggle behind it.
